coredata.dc:title: Poly(β-cyclodextrin)/carbon quantum dots modified glassy carbon electrode: Preparation, characterization and simultaneous electrochemical determination of dopamine, uric acid and tryptophan
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jingchao
He, Ping
Bai, Hongmei
He, Shaoying
Zhang, Tinghong
Zhang, Xingquan
Dong, Faqin
coredata.dc:description: A novel poly(β-cyclodextrin) (β-CD)/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) composite based sensor was successfully fabricated on glassy carbon electrode through electrochemical polymerization. As-prepared β-CD/CQDs modified electrode was used for the simultaneous determination of dopamine (DA), uric acid (UA) and tryptophan (Trp). Electrochemical responses of as-prepared electrode were investigated by cyclic voltammetry, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and differential pulse voltammetry. In co-existence system containing DA, UA and Trp, three oxidation peaks well separated from each other and the potential separations of oxidation peaks of DA-UA and UA-Trp were respectively large up to 150 and 420mV, owing to the synergistic effect of β-CD and CQDs. The linear amperometric responses for DA, UA and Trp were obtained in the concentration range of 4–220, 0.3–200 and 5–270μM, respectively, with the limits of detection of 0.14, 0.01, 0.16μM (S/N=3) and the limits of quantification of 0.45, 0.04, 0.50μM (S/N=10). Moreover, as-prepared β-CD/CQDs modified electrode exhibited prominent selectivity, stability and reproducibility, which had promising application for the simultaneous determination of DA, UA and Trp.
coredata.dcterms:subject: β-Cyclodextrin
Carbon quantum dots
Modified electrode
Dopamine
Uric acid
Tryptophan

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of as-prepared materials and modified electrodes
Characterization of CQDs and as-prepared composite
Electrocatalytic oxidation of DA, UA and Trp
Effect of pH on the simultaneous determination of DA, UA and Trp
Effect of scan rate on the simultaneous determination of DA, UA and Trp
Electrochemical determination of DA, UA and Trp
Stability and reproducibility of as-prepared electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD (A) and UV–vis absorption (B) spectrum of CQDs.
FTIR spectra of CQDs (a), β-CD (b) and β-CD/CQDs (c).
TEM image of CQDs (A), SEM images of GCE (B) and β-CD/CQDs/GCE (C).
CVs for electropolymerization of β-CD in 0.10M PBS (containing β-cyclodextrin+CQDs). Scan rate: 50mVs−1.
CVs of 1.1×10−4 M DA+1.5×10−4 M UA+2.5×10−4 M Trp in 0.10M PBS on GCE (a), β-CD/GCE (b), CQDs/GCE (c) and β-CD/CQDs/GCE (d), respectively. Scan rate: 50mVs−1.

CVs of 4.0×10−6 M DA, 5.5×10−5 M UA and 8.5×10−5 M Trp in 0.10M PBS with different pH on β-CD/CQDs/GCE (A) at scan rate of 50mVs−1. Effect of pH on peak potentials (B) and peak currents (C) for oxidation of DA, UA and Trp.

DPVs of 1.0×10−5 M UA, 6.2×10−6 M Trp and varying concentrations of DA in 0.10M PBS on β-CD/CQDs/GCE (A); DPVs of 3.6×10−5 M DA, 2.3×10−5 M Trp in 0.10M PBS and varying concentrations of UA on β-CD/CQDs/GCE (B); DPVs of 3.4×10−5 M DA, 6.3×10−6 M UA and varying concentrations of Trp in 0.10M PBS on β-CD/CQDs/GCE (C). Insets were the linear calibration plots of currents versus concentrations. Increment potential of each point=0.004V, potential pulse amplitude=0.05V, potential pulse width=0.05s, data sampling width=0.0167s and potential pulse period=0.2s.
Comparative analytical performance of β-CD/CQDs/GCE with other reported electrode for the simultaneous determination of DA, UA and Trp.
Determination of DA, UA and Trp in urine samples on β-CD/CQDs/GCE (n =5).

head.ce:title: Poly(β-cyclodextrin)/carbon quantum dots modified glassy carbon electrode: Preparation, characterization and simultaneous electrochemical determination of dopamine, uric acid and tryptophan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest University of Science and Technology, Mianyang 621010, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Southwest University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Analysis and Test, Southwest University of Science and Technology, Mianyang 621010, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Southwest University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Solid Waste Treatment and Resource Recycle of Ministry of Education, Mianyang 621010, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Solid Waste Treatment and Resource Recycle of Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel poly(β-cyclodextrin) (β-CD)/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) composite based sensor was successfully fabricated on glassy carbon electrode through electrochemical polymerization. As-prepared β-CD/CQDs modified electrode was used for the simultaneous determination of dopamine (DA), uric acid (UA) and tryptophan (Trp). Electrochemical responses of as-prepared electrode were investigated by cyclic voltammetry, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and differential pulse voltammetry. In co-existence system containing DA, UA and Trp, three oxidation peaks well separated from each other and the potential separations of oxidation peaks of DA-UA and UA-Trp were respectively large up to 150 and 420mV, owing to the synergistic effect of β-CD and CQDs. The linear amperometric responses for DA, UA and Trp were obtained in the concentration range of 4–220, 0.3–200 and 5–270μM, respectively, with the limits of detection of 0.14, 0.01, 0.16μM (S/N=3) and the limits of quantification of 0.45, 0.04, 0.50μM (S/N=10). Moreover, as-prepared β-CD/CQDs modified electrode exhibited prominent selectivity, stability and reproducibility, which had promising application for the simultaneous determination of DA, UA and Trp.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA), one of the excitatory neurotransmitters, plays an essential role in several physiological events such as central nervous, renal and hormonal systems as well as in drug addiction and Parkinson's disease [1]. Uric acid (UA) is the main final product of purine metabolism in humans and an important marker in clinical diagnosis of diseases, such as cardiovascular, neurological, hypertension and renal insufficiency diseases [2]. As precursor of the neurotransmitter serotonin, tryptophan (Trp) is one of amino acids which are essential to human life. It has been implicated as a possible cause of schizophrenia in people who cannot properly metabolize it [3].

DA, UA and Trp usually coexist together in biological samples, so that establishing a selective and sensitive sensor for the simultaneous determination of DA, UA and Trp is highly desirable in analytical application and diagnostic research [4]. In recent years, several methods have been developed to simultaneously detect two or more components of DA, UA and Trp, such as spectrophotometric method, high performance liquid chromatography, surface plasmon resonance, capillary electrophoresis and electrochemical method [3,5–10]. Among these methods, electrochemical method has attracted special attention due to the characteristics of fast detection, low cost, easy operation and high efficiency [11]. Because of the overlap of voltammetric responses of DA, UA and Trp, the simultaneous determination of them is highly difficult [12]. Therefore, great efforts have been given to develop desirable electrode materials for the simultaneous determination of DA, UA and Trp [13].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel zero-dimensional carbon nano-material with size below 10nm, have excellent physical and chemical properties, including low toxicity, favorite redox properties, simple synthetic routes and high stability [14]. Because of these advantageous characteristics, CQDs have obtained tremendous attention for potential applications in the field of photocatalysis, fluorescent bioimaging and electrochemistry, etc. [15–17]. As for sensors, CQDs were successfully used to detect inorganic ions, organisms and biomolecules, etc [18–20]. Hence, it would be interesting to utilize CQDs on modified electrode for electrochemical sensor.

β-cyclodextrin is an oligosaccharides consisting of seven glucose units, which presents a toroidal form with a hydrophobic inner cavity and a hydrophilic outer side [21]. β-cyclodextrin has high molecular selectivity and enantioselectivity, making it widely used as molecular receptors and particularly applied in many fields, such as medicine, daily chemical industry and electrochemical sensor [22]. Due to excellent properties of β-cyclodextrin, some electrochemical sensors based on poly(β-cyclodextrin) (β-CD) have been reported, such as UA, fluoroquinolones and gatifloxacin, etc. 23–25]. During electrocatalytic reactions, the object, as a guest molecule, forms inclusion complexes with β-CD through hydrophobic and hydrogen-bonding interaction. Therefore, β-CD acts as modifiers and can improve the sensitivity and selectivity of sensor.

We successfully developed a highly sensitive sensor based on β-CD and CQDs towards the simultaneous electrochemical determination of DA, UA and Trp. As-prepared electrode presented satisfactory selectivity, excellent reproducibility and long-time stability because of the synergistic effect of β-CD and CQDs. Separations of DA-UA and UA-Trp oxidation peaks potentials were 150 and 420mV, which were large enough to realize the simultaneous electrochemical determination of DA, UA and Trp.

Citric acid (CA), β-cyclodextrin, l-cysteine, glutathione and folic acid were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., (Shanghai, China). DA, UA, Trp and glucose were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, USA). K3Fe(CN)6, KCl, NaOH, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, NaCl, KNO3, CaCl2, MgSO4 and ZnCl2 were analytical grade and purchased from Chengdu Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was prepared by mixed stock solutions of 0.10M Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4. The stock solutions of DA, UA and Trp were prepared daily and kept in an ice bath. Doubly distilled water was used throughout the whole experiment. High purity nitrogen (99.999%) was used for deaeration before electrochemical detection.

Based on easy bottom-up method, CQDs were directly prepared by pyrolysing CA [26]. 2.50g CA was heated and melted in oil bath at 180°C. After 30min of heating, the melted CA liquid became orange, suggesting the formation of CQDs. Afterwards, the melted CA liquid was slowly added in 125mL NaOH solution (10.0gL−1) with vigorous stirring and adjusted pH to 7.0. The as-prepared CQDs were diluted to 400mL with doubly distilled water and refrigerated at 3°C.

Prior to modification, glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was polished with 500 and 50nm aluminum oxide powders to a mirror-like and then washed successively with ethanol and doubly distilled water for several times.

A homogeneous mixture was formed by adding 7.1mg β-cyclodextrin and 5.0mL CQDs into 25mL 0.10M PBS, and sonicated for at least 30min to obtain β-cyclodextrin/CQDs mixture solution. The preparation of β-CD/CQDs/GCE was carried out by cyclic voltammetry (CV) between −1.0–1.0V at scan rate of 50mVs−1 for 4 cycles in β-cyclodextrin/CQDs mixture solution. As-prepared electrode was thoroughly washed with doubly distilled water carefully and dried in air. For comparison, the preparation procedures of β-CD/GCE and CQDs/GCE were similar to that of β-CD/CQDs/GCE, which was carried out by CV in β-cyclodextrin or CQDs mixture solution. Before all electrochemical measurements, the electrolytes were purged with N2 for 5min to remove oxygen completely.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of as-prepared materials and modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The structure of CQDs was investigated by X-ray diffractometer (X’ Pert PRO, Netherlands) with Cu K α radiation (k =0.154060nm) and recorded from 10° to 60° at a speed of 2.0°/min. UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs was recorded on UV–vis spectrometer (Specord 200PLUS, Analytik Jena AG). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of CQDs, β-CD and β-CD/CQDs were recorded with FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet-5700, USA) in wavenumber range of 4000–900cm−1 using KBr sheets. The morphologies of CQDs and β-CD/CQDs composite were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM, Libra 200FE, Carl Zeiss) at an acceleration voltage of 200kV and scanning electron microscope (SEM, Ultra 55, Carl Zeiss) at an acceleration voltage of 15kV, respectively.

The data for CV, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and differential pulse voltammogram (DPV) were obtained with PARSTAT 2273 electrochemical workstation (Princeton Applied Research, USA) with a three-electrode test system using a platinum electrode as counter electrode, a bare or modified GCE as working electrode referred to saturated calomel electrode (SCE).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs and as-prepared composite

ce:section.ce:para: Shown in Fig. 1 A was XRD spectrum of CQDs. A broad peak of CQDs at around 18.06° was observed, which corresponded to the characteristic lattice plane (002) [16]. Shown in Fig. 1B was UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs. It was observed that CQDs had two absorption bands: one at 240nm due to the formation of multiple polyaromatic chromophores and the other one at 360nm due to n-π* transition of CQDs [27].

Shown in Fig. 2 were FTIR spectra of CQDs, β-CD and β-CD/CQDs. It was observed in curve a that the broad absorption band at 3700–3100cm−1 was assigned to stretching vibrations of OH. And the bands at 1676 and 1250cm−1 were attributed to the vibration absorptions of CC and COH groups, respectively, implying the existence of large numbers of residual hydroxyl groups. These functional groups were favorable to improve hydrophilicity and stability of CQDs in an aqueous system [28]. Curve b showed characteristic FTIR spectrum of electropolymerized β-CD, where the ‘fingerprint’ region of β-CD (wavenumber range: 1200–980cm−1) was clearly observed [29]. The intense peaks at 1638, 2925 and the broad band peak at 3457cm−1 corresponded to CO, CH2 and OH stretching vibrations, respectively [30,31].

As shown in the spectrum of β-CD/CQDs (curve c), absorption peaks were observed at 3000–2800 and 3600–3100cm−1, which related to CH and OH stretching vibration of β-CD. Compared with curve b, all absorption peaks of β-CD were found in β-CD/CQDs composite, indicating that β-CD was attached with CQDs. In additions, the intensity of absorption peaks for CO and OH of as-prepared composite were much stronger than that of β-CD or CQDs, which identified as the interactions between β-CD and CQDs. Also, it was indicated that CQDs successfully attached to β-CD. The results proved that GCE was successfully modified with β-CD/CQDs [32].

Shown in Fig. 3 A was TEM image of CQDs and the individual CQDs structural unit was clearly observed. The shape of CQDs was spherical with a diameter about 7.6nm. Shown in Fig. 3B was SEM image of bare GCE. Compared with Fig. 3B, the loose and porous surface was observed on β-CD/CQDs/GCE (Fig. 3C), which was fabricated by CV scans in the mixture solution containing β-cyclodextrin and CQDs. The result further proved that GCE was successfully modified with β-CD/CQDs and electrocatalytic activity of as-prepared electrode was promoted.

Shown in Fig. 4 were CVs for electropolymerization of β-CD at scan rate of 50mVs−1 in 0.10M PBS containing 7.1mg β-cyclodextrin and 5.0mL CQDs. The adsorbed β-CD molecules on electrode showed two electrochemical processes at −0.025 and 0.380V and the peaks currents on successive scanning changed in the range of −1.0 to 1.0V. The reason was that the production of cationic radicals of the β-CD exhibited redox properties on electrode surface [33]. In addition, after 4 cycles of potential scanning, the peaks currents tended to be stable, indicating the complete formation of conducting polymeric film β-CD/CQDs on electrode surface.

The thickness and uniformity of β-CD/CQDs film could be facilely controlled through regulating the scan cycles during electropolymerization process. Shown in Fig. S1 were CVs of K3Fe(CN)6 solution on β-CD/CQDs/GCE prepared with different scan cycles (1–8 cycles). The redox peaks currents decreased over 4 cycles, suggesting that more cycles led to the formation of thicker film and the probe of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− could not arrive on electrode surface because of high mass transfer resistance. Therefore, electropolymerization of 4 CV cycles were used as optimum condition for the construction of β-CD/CQDs modified electrode.

Shown in Fig. S2 were CVs of K3Fe(CN)6 solution on bare GCE and as-prepared electrodes. Compared with bare GCE (curve a), the peaks currents of β-CD/GCE (curve b) and CQDs/GCE (curve c) increased, suggesting that β-CD or CQDs was beneficial to facilitate electron transfer between [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and electrode. In addition, the smallest ΔE p was obtained for [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution on as-prepared β-CD/CQDs/GCE (curve d). After the combination of β-CD and CQDs, as-prepared β-CD/CQDs/GCE showed the highest redox peak current, which demonstrated that two materials in the composite had synergic effect for facilitating electron transfer between [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and β-CD/CQDs/GCE.

Shown in Fig. S3 were Nyquist plots of K3Fe(CN)6 solution on bare GCE, β-CD/GCE, CQDs/GCE and β-CD/CQDs/GCE. The bare GCE (curve a) exhibited an almost straight line at low frequency region, which corresponded to the diffusion process. The semicircle-like shape with larger diameter was observed on β-CD/GCE (curve b) or CQDs/GCE (curve c), indicating that β-CD or CQDs was successfully assembled on electrode surface and larger increase in resistance was achieved due to the hindrance to electron-transfer kinetics. Decrease of the charge transfer resistance was observed when β-CD/CQDs composite (curve d) modified on electrode surface. As-prepared composite charge transfer reduced the ion intercalation distances and facilitated efficient charge transfers and lower resistances, which confirmed the CV results and further proved that β-CD/CQDs composite was more suitable as electrode materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic oxidation of DA, UA and Trp
ce:section.ce:para: DA, UA and Trp coexisted in physiological fluids, and the potentials of oxidation peaks of them were very close, resulting in overlapped voltammetric responses on bare electrode. Hence, the selectivity was important when the electrochemical determinations of DA, UA and Trp were built.

Shown in Fig. 5 were CVs of DA, UA and Trp in 0.10M PBS on GCE, β-CD/GCE, CQDs/GCE and β-CD/CQDs/GCE. As observed in Fig. 5a, it was difficult to locate the potentials of oxidation peaks individually of three components (DA, UA and Trp) in 0.10M PBS on bare GCE. Conversely, three well defined and separated oxidation peaks corresponding to DA, UA and Trp were observed in Fig. 5b–d. The current responses of DA, UA and Trp enhanced on β-CD/CQDs/GCE compared to that on β-CD/GCE or CQDs/GCE. The potentials of oxidation peaks of DA, UA and Trp on β-CD/CQDs/GCE were located at 0.16, 0.31 and 0.73V (vs. SCE), and the peak separations of DA-UA and UA-Trp oxidation potentials were 150 and 420mV, respectively. Therefore, it was successful to achieve the simultaneous determination of DA, UA and Trp on as-prepared β-CD/CQDs/GCE.

Shown in Fig. S4 were CVs of 5.0×10−6 M DA, 6.0×10−5 M UA and 2.0×10−4 M Trp on GCE or β-CD/CQDs/GCE in 0.10M PBS, respectively. As observed in Fig. S4A, the peaks of DA, UA and Trp were rather broad due to slow process of electron transfer, suggesting that it was difficult to achieve the simultaneous determination of DA, UA and Trp on bare GCE. As for β-CD/CQDs/GCE (Fig. S4B), sharp redox peaks of three compounds were found. The potentials of oxidation peaks of DA and UA were observed at 0.15 and 0.30V (vs. SCE), respectively, and the peak separation of DA-UA oxidation potential was large up to 150mV (curve a′ and b′). Meanwhile, the oxidation peak current of Trp increased on β-CD/CQDs/GCE (curve c′) compared to that on bare GCE. It was indicated that as-prepared β-CD/CQDs/GCE exhibited electrocatalytic properties towards oxidation of DA, UA and Trp.

A possible reaction mechanism of β-CD/CQDs with electrocatalytic oxidation was discussed. The observed enhancement was attributed to increase of the electroactive surface area with CQDs and effective electronic coupling of β-CD/CQDs and objects. CQDs could make the electrode surface more conductive, and due to carboxyl groups of CQDs with negative charge, β-CD/CQDs not only had good stability but also enabled interaction with objects through electrostatic and □-□ stacking interaction [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the simultaneous determination of DA, UA and Trp
ce:section.ce:para: The pH value of the supporting electrolyte is an important factor that affects the electrochemical reactions of analytes [35]. The effect of pH on the simultaneous determination of DA, UA and Trp with as-prepared β-CD/CQDs/GCE was studied in 0.10M PBS with pH in the range of 5.0–8.0, which was shown in Fig. 6 .

As shown in Fig. 6A, oxidation peaks shifted negatively with increasing pH value, indicating that protons were involved in the electrode reaction. The relationships between pH and potentials of oxidation peaks of DA, UA and Trp were shown in Fig. 6B. The linear relationships were expressed by regression equations of E pa,DA/(V)=(0.512±0.007) – (0.050±0.001) pH, E pa,UA/(V)=(0.635±0.010) – (0.048±0.001) pH, E pa,Trp/(V)=(0.976±0.026) – (0.043±0.004) pH with correlation coefficients of 0.997, 0.994 and 0.953, respectively. The slopes of DA, UA and Trp were close to the Nernst value of 59mVpH−1 for a two proton and two electron transfer process [36]. The relationships between pH and oxidation peaks currents of DA, UA and Trp were shown in Fig. 6C. The maximum peak current of DA was observed at pH 6.5, on the other hand, the oxidation peaks currents of UA and Trp decreased gradually with pH value from 5.5 to 8.0. Hence, pH 6.5 was selected for the simultaneous electrochemical determination of DA, UA and Trp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of scan rate on the simultaneous determination of DA, UA and Trp
ce:section.ce:para: Shown in Fig. S5 were CVs of 0.10M PBS (pH 6.5) containing DA, UA and Trp on as-prepared β-CD/CQDs/GCE with different scan rates. The good linear relationships were obtained between peaks currents and scan rates from 10 to 600mVs−1 (Fig. S6). The linear relationships were expressed by the following regression equations: for DA (Fig. S6A), I pa,DA/(μA)=(5.050±0.413)+(0.046±0.001) v/(mVs−1); for UA (Fig. S6B), I pa,UA/(μA)=(5.008±0.370)+(0.047±0.001) v/(mVs−1) and for Trp (Fig. S6C), I pa,Trp/(μA)=(4.936±0.362)+(0.047±0.001) v/(mVs−1) with correlation coefficients of 0.992, 0.994 and 0.994, respectively, demonstrating that the electrochemical behaviors of DA, UA and Trp on as-prepared β-CD/CQDs/GCE were adsorption-controlled processes [22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical determination of DA, UA and Trp

ce:section.ce:para: Compared with CV, DPV has much higher current sensitivity and better resolution. Shown in Fig. 7 were DPVs of the simultaneous determination of DA, UA and Trp in their mixtures in which concentration of one substance changed, while the other two species remained constant. The concentration change of one compound had no significant influence of other two compounds, indicating that the responses for DA, UA and Trp on as-prepared β-CD/CQDs/GCE were relatively independent.

As observed in Fig. 7A, the peak currents of DA increased linearly with the increase of DA concentration from 4 to 220μM in the mixture of UA and Trp. The linear regression equation was expressed as I pa,DA/(μA)=(9.611±0.714)+(0.288±0.006) c/(μM) with correlation coefficient of 0.996. Similarly, observed in Fig. 7B, the peak currents of UA increased linearly with the increase of UA concentration from 0.3 to 200μM in the mixture of DA and Trp. The linear regression equation was expressed as I pa,UA/(μA)=(4.577±0.473)+(0.239±0.004) c/(μM) with correlation coefficient of 0.997. As for Fig. 7C, the peak currents of Trp increased linearly with the increase of Trp concentration from 5 to 270μM in the mixture of DA and UA. The linear regression equation was expressed as I pa,Trp/(μA)=(5.594±0.469)+(0.155±0.003) c/(μM) with a correlation coefficient of 0.997. Based on the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N), the limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) were calculated to be 0.14, 0.01, 0.16μM (S/N=3) and 0.45, 0.04, 0.50μM (S/N=10), respectively, for DA, UA and Trp.

To further demonstrate the feasibility of the simultaneous determination of DA, UA and Trp on as-prepared β-CD/CQDs/GCE, the determination of each compound alone was carried out by DPV in 0.10M PBS (Fig. S7), where insets were the linear calibration plots of currents versus concentrations. The linear regression equation for DA was expressed as I pa,DA/(μA)=(8.120±0.826)+(0.305±0.008) c/(μM) with correlation coefficient of 0.993 (Fig. S7A); for UA, I pa,UA/(μA)=(5.158±0.579)+(0.226±0.005) c/(μM) with correlation coefficient of 0.995 (Fig. S7B); for Trp, I pa,Trp/(μA)=(6.642±0.801)+(0.153±0.008) c/(μM) with correlation coefficient of 0.996 (Fig. S7C). The slopes of linear regression equations of each compound alone were very close to those of each compound in the mixture, showing similar sensitivities for the determination of DA, UA and Trp alone or in the mixture. The comparison of sensitivity was presented in Table S1. The results further indicated that the simultaneous determination of DA, UA and Trp was feasible in mixture solution.

Moreover, comparison of other sensors performances which employed different materials was presented in Table 1 . It was found that as-prepared β-CD/CQDs/GCE exhibited satisfactory linear range and detection limit for the simultaneous determination of DA, UA and Trp.

In order to evaluate the anti-interferential ability of β-CD/CQDs/GCE, the interfering effects were investigated in 0.10M PBS (pH 6.5) containing 1.1×10−4 M DA, 1.5×10−4 M UA, 2.5×10−4 M Trp and the following potential components: Zn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, NO3 −, glucose, CA, l-cysteine, glutathione and folic acid. As observed in Table S2, it was found that no significant interference (signal changes below 5%) was observed, indicating that the proposed sensor was selective for the simultaneous determination of DA, UA and Trp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and reproducibility of as-prepared electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Stability and reproducibility were further evaluated performance of as-prepared β-CD/CQDs/GCE for the simultaneous determination of DA, UA and Trp. The as-prepared electrode was stored in 0.10M PBS at 4°C in refrigerator when it was not used. The stability of as-prepared β-CD/CQDs/GCE was evaluated by measuring the current responses for the mixtures of DA (1.1×10−4 M), UA (1.5×10−4 M) and Trp (2.5×10−4 M) over 2 weeks. The peaks currents gradually decreased, but no obvious changes were noticed after storage and the responses maintained 93.5%, 96.2% and 94.7% for DA, UA and Trp of the initial currents, respectively. Additionally, twenty measurements for DA, UA and Trp on as-prepared β-CD/CQDs/GCE were carried out and relative standard deviations for all three species were less than 5% (Fig. S8), confirming that as-prepared electrode had satisfactory reproducibility. These results demonstrated that stability and reproducibility of as-prepared β-CD/CQDs/GCE were satisfactory.

Urine sample was selected for analysis by the proposed method using the standard addition technique. Before measurement, urine samples were diluted 20 times with 0.10M PBS. Different amounts of DA, UA and Trp were added into the sample solution to test recovery rates, and the results were shown in Table 2 . It was observed that the recovery rates of DA, UA and Trp were 97–104%, 98–105% and 98–104%, respectively. Therefore, the standard addition was successful with satisfactory results.

In this work, β-CD/CQDs composite modified electrode was successfully constructed through facile green process and used for the simultaneous determination of DA, UA and Trp. As-prepared β-CD/CQDs/GCE exhibited high selectivity, fast electron transfer, long-time stability and excellent reproducibility for the oxidation of DA, UA and Trp. Simultaneous measurements of three analytes were finished successfully on β-CD/CQDs/GCE without any interference. The currents of oxidation peaks showed linear dependent on the concentrations of DA, UA and Trp, respectively. Moreover, the proposed method was an attractive candidate for the simultaneous determination of DA, UA and Trp in practical applications with satisfactory results.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science & Technology Supported Program (2014BAC13B05) and the National Basic Research Program of China (2014CB846003). Also we are grateful for the help of Analytical and Testing Center of Southwest University of Science and Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.05.096.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dopamine biosensor based on surface functionalized nanostructured nickel oxide platform
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jingchao Chen is currently pursuing for her master degree under the supervision of Professor Ping He in Southwest University of Science and Technology, majoring in electrochemistry sensors, especially biosensors, and she has published papers in Microchimica Acta, Journal of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Journal of Colloid and Interface Science, Applied Surface Science and Water Science and Technology.

Ping He is a chemistry and materials professor of School of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest University of Science and Technology, PR China. He received his doctor degree in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, PR China, in 2005. His research areas include electrochemistry, materials chemistry and analytical chemistry, especially in electrode materials, etc. Prof Ping He has published more than 110 papers in Electrochimica Acta, Chemical Communications, Electrochemistry Communications, Journal of Power Sources, Journal of Materials Chemistry A, Journal of Electroanalytical Chemistry and Composites Science and Technology, etc.

Hongmei Bai is a master candidate of Southwest University of Science and Technology. She majors in materials electrochemistry under the supervision of Professor Ping He. She has published papers in Applied Surface Science, Journal of Colloid Interface and Science and Journal of Electroanalytical Chemistry.

Shaoying He is currently pursuing for her master degree under the supervision of Professor Ping He in Southwest University of Science and Technology, majoring in analytical chemistry. She is interested in the synthesis of nanomaterials and the application in electrochemistry sensors, especially biosensors.

Tinghong Zhang is an associate professor of School of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest University of Science and Technology. His current research interests include nano-materials, functional inorganic materials and fluorescent materials. He has published more than 60 papers.

Xingquan Zhang is a materials associate professor of Analytical and Testing Center, Southwest University of Science and Technology. He received his doctor degree in Harbin Institute of Technology (HIT), PR China, in 2011. His research interests include materials chemistry and functional materials. Dr. Xingquan Zhang has published more than 40 papers in Journal of Alloys and Compounds, Journal of Applied Physics and Journal of Nanomaterials, etc.

Faqin Dong is a professor of Key Laboratory of Solid Waste Treatment and Resource Recycle of Ministry of Education, Southwest University of Science and Technology. He received his doctor degree in China University of Geosciences, PR China, in 1992. His research interests are materials chemistry and environmental chemistry. Prof. Faqin Dong has published more than 160 papers.
